The conjugate tramsose of a matrix plays a very important role in the remainder of this chapter. In the case that $A$ has real entries; $A^{*}$ is simply the transpose of $A$.

## Example 5

Let $V=M_{n \times n}(F)$, and define $\langle A . B\rangle=\operatorname{tr}\left\langle B^{*} A\right)$ for $A . B \in V$. (Recall that the trace of a matrix $A$ is defined by $\operatorname{tr}(A)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{i i}$.) We verify that (a) and (d) of the definition of imer product hold and leave (b) and (c) to the reader. For this purpose, let $A, B, C \in V$. Then (using Exercise 6 of Section 1.3)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle A+B \cdot C\rangle & =\operatorname{tr}\left(C^{*}(A+B)\right)=\operatorname{tr}\left(C^{*} A+C^{*} B\right) \\
& =\operatorname{tr}\left(C^{*} A\right)+\operatorname{tr}\left(C^{*} B\right)=\left\langle A, C^{*}\right\rangle+\langle B ; C\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Also

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle A, A\rangle & =\operatorname{tr}\left(A^{*} A\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(A^{*} A\right)_{i i}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(A^{*}\right)_{i k} A_{k ; j} \\
& =\sum_{i-1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \overline{A_{k ;}} A_{k i}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n}\left|A_{k i}\right|^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now if $A \neq O$, then $A_{k i} \neq 0$ for some $k$ and $i$. So $\langle A, A\rangle>0$.

The inner product on $\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)$ in Example 5 is called the Frobenius inner product.

A vector space V over $F$ endowed with a specific inner product is called an inner product space. If $F=C$, we call $\vee$ a complex inner product space, whereas if $F=R$, we call $\vee$ a real inner product space.

It is clear that if V has an inner product $\langle x, y\rangle$ and W is a subspace of V , then W is also an inner product space when the same function $\langle x, y\rangle$ is restricted to the vectors $x, y \in \mathrm{~W}$.

Thus Examples 1, 3, and 5 also provide examples of inner product spaces. For the remainder of this chapter, $\mathrm{F}^{n}$ denotes the inner product space with the standard inner product as defined in Example 1. Likewise, $\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)$ denotes the inner product space with the Frobenius inner product as defined in Example 5. The reader is cautioned that two distinct inner products on a given vector space yield two distinct inner product spaces. For instance, it can be shown that both

$$
\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle_{1}=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t \quad \text { and } \quad\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle_{2}=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t
$$

are inner products on the vector space $\mathrm{P}(R)$. Even though the underlying vector space is the same, however, these two inner products yield two different inner product spaces. For example, the polynomials $f(x)=x$ and $g(x)=x^{2}$ are orthogonal in the second inner product space, but not in the first.

A very important inner product space that resembles $C([0,1])$ is the space $H$ of continuous complex-valued functions defined on the interval $[0,2 \pi]$ with the inner product

$$
\langle f, g\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) \overline{g(t)} d t
$$

The reason for the constant $1 / 2 \pi$ will become evident later. This inner product space, which arises often in the context of physical situations, is examined more closely in later sections.

At this point, we mention a few facts about integration of complex-valued functions. First, the imaginary number $i$ can be treated as a constant under the integration sign. Sccond, every complex-valued function $f$ may be written as $f=f_{1}+i f_{2}$, where $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ are real-valued functions. Thus we have

$$
\int f=\int f_{1}+i \int f_{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \overline{\int f}=\int \bar{f}
$$

From these properties, as well as the assumption of continuity, it follows that H is an inner product space (see Exercise 16(a)).

Some properties that follow easily from the definition of an inner product are contained in the next theorem.

Theorem 6.1. Let $\vee$ be an inner product space. Then for $x, y, z \in \mathrm{~V}$ and $c \in F$. the following statements are true.
(a) $\langle x, y+z\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle+\langle x, z\rangle$.
(b) $\langle x, c y\rangle=\bar{c}\langle x, y\rangle$.
(c) $\langle x, 0\rangle=\langle 0, x\rangle=0$.
(d) $\langle x . x\rangle=0$ if and only if $x=0$.
(e) If $\langle r, y\rangle=\langle x, z\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$, then $y=z$.

Proof. (a) We have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle x, y+z\rangle & =\overline{\langle y+z, x\rangle}=\overline{\langle y, x\rangle+\langle z, r\rangle} \\
& =\overline{\langle y, x\rangle}+\overline{\langle z, x,}=\langle x, y\rangle+\langle x, z\rangle .
\end{aligned}
$$

The proofs of (b). (c). (d) and (c) are left as exereises.
The reader should observe that (a) and (b) of Theorem (i.l show that the immer product is conjugate linear in the second component.

In order to generalize the notion of length in $R^{3}$ to arbitrary inmer product spares. we med only ohserve that the length of $x=(a, b, c) \in \mathrm{R}^{3}$ is given by $\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+t^{2}}=\sqrt{\langle r, x\rangle}$. This leads to the following definition.

Definition. Let $V$ be an imur product space. For $r \in V$, we define the norm or length of.r $b, v\|: x\|=\sqrt{\langle\cdot r \cdot x\rangle}$.

## Example 6

Let $V=F^{\prime \prime}$. If $r=\left(a_{1} . a_{2} \ldots a_{n}\right)$, then

$$
\|\cdot x\|=\left\|\left(a_{1}, a_{2} \ldots, a_{n}\right)\right\|=\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

is the Euclidean definition of length. Note that if $n=1$. we have $\|a\|=|a|$.

As we might expert, the well-known properties of Euclidean length in $R^{3}$ hold in general. as shown next.

Theorem 6.2. Let $V$ be an inncr product spater over $F$. Then for all $r . y \in \mathrm{~V}$ and,$\in \in F$, the following statrments are true.
(a) $\|c x\|=|c| \cdot\|\cdot r\|$.
(b) $\|r\|=0$ if and only if $. x=0$. In muy cass, $\|r\| \geq 0$.
(c) (C'anchus Schwazz Incquality) $|\langle x, y\rangle| \leq\|x\| \cdot\|!\| \|$.
(d) (Triangle Inequality) $\|x+y\| \leq\|\cdot\|\|+\| y \|$.

Proof. We leave the proofs of (a) and (b) as exercises.
(c) If $y=0$, then the result is immediate. So assume that $y \neq 0$. For any $c \in F$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \leq\|x-c y\|^{2} & =\langle x-c y, x-c y\rangle=\langle x, x-c y\rangle-c\langle y, x-c y\rangle \\
& =\langle x, x\rangle-\bar{c}\langle x, y\rangle-c\langle y, x\rangle+c \bar{c}\langle y . y\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

In particular, if we set

$$
c=\frac{\langle x, y\rangle}{\langle y, y\rangle},
$$

the inequality becomes

$$
0 \leq\langle x, x\rangle-\frac{|\langle x . y\rangle|^{2}}{\langle y, y\rangle}=\|x\|^{2}-\frac{|\langle x, y\rangle|^{2}}{\|y\|^{2}}
$$

from which (c) follows.
(d) We have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|x+y\|^{2} & =\langle x+y, x+y\rangle=\langle x, x\rangle+\langle y, x\rangle+\langle x, y\rangle+\langle y, y\rangle \\
& =\|x\|^{2}+2 \Re\langle x, y\rangle+\|y\|^{2} \\
& \leq\|x\|^{2}+2|\langle x, y\rangle|+\|y\|^{2} \\
& \leq\|x\|^{2}+i\|x\| \cdot\|y\|+\|y\|^{2} \\
& =(\|x\|+\|y\|)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Re\langle x, y\rangle$ denotes the real part of the complex number $\langle x, y\rangle$. Note that we used (c) to prove (d).

The case when equality results in (c) and (d) is considered in Exercise 15.

## Example 7

For $\mathrm{F}^{n}$., we may apply (c) and (d) of Theorem 6.2 to the staudard inner product to obtain the following well-known incqualities:

$$
\left|\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} \bar{b}_{i}\right| \leq\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|b_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

and

$$
\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}+b_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2} \leq\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}+\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|b_{i}\right|^{2}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

The reader may recall from earlier courses that, for $x$ and $y$ in $\mathrm{R}^{3}$ or $\mathrm{R}^{2}$, we have that $\langle x, y\rangle=\|x\| \cdot\|y\| \cos \theta$, where $\theta(0 \leq \theta \leq \pi)$ denotes the angle between $x$ and $y$. This equation implies (c) immediately since $|\cos \theta| \leq 1$. Notice also that nonzero vectors $x$ and $y$ are perpendicular if and only if $\cos \theta=0$, that is. if and only if $\langle x, y\rangle=0$.

We are now at the point where we can generalize the notion of perpendicularity to arbitrary inner product spaces.

Definitions. Let $V$ be an inner product space. Vectors $x$ and $y$ in $V$ are orthogonal (perpendicular) if $\langle x, y\rangle=0$. A subset $S$ of V is orthogonal if any two distinct vectors in $S$ are orthogonal. A vector $x$ in $V$ is a unit vector if $\|x\|=1$. Finally, a subsct $S$ of V is orthonormal if $S$ is orthogonal and consists cutirely of unit vectors.

Note that if $S=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots\right\}$, then $S$ is orthonormal if and only if $\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=$ $\delta_{i j}$, where $\delta_{i j}$ denotes the Kronecker delta. Also, obscrve that multiplying vectors by nonzero scalars does not affect their orthogonality and that if $x$ is any nonzero vector, then $(1 /\|x\|) x$ is a unit vector. The process of multiplying a nonzero vector by the reciprocal of its length is called normalizing.

## Example 8

In $\mathrm{F}^{3},\{(1,1,0),(1,-1,1),(-1,1,2)\}$ is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors, but it is not orthonormal; however, if we normalize the vectors in the set, we obtain the orthonormal set

$$
\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1,0), \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,-1,1), \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-1,1,2)\right\}
$$

Our next example is of an infinite orthonormal set that is important in analysis. This set is used in later examples in this chapter.

## Example 9

Recall the inner product space H (defined on page 332). We introduce an important orthonormal subset $S$ of H . For what follows, $i$ is the imaginary number such that $i^{2}=-1$. For any integer $n$, let $f_{n}(t)=e^{i n t}$, where $0 \leq t \leq 2 \pi$. (Recall that $e^{i n t}=\cos n t+i \sin n t$.) Now definc $S=\left\{f_{n}: n\right.$ is an integer $\}$. Clearly $S$ is a subset of H . Using the property that $\overline{e^{i t}}=e^{-i t}$ for cvery real number $t$, we have, for $m \neq n$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle f_{m}, f_{n}\right\rangle & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} e^{i m t} \overline{e^{i n t}} d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} e^{i(m-n) t} d t \\
& =\left.\frac{1}{2 \pi i(m-n)} e^{i(m-n) t}\right|_{0} ^{2 \pi}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Also,

$$
\left\langle f_{n} \cdot f_{n}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} e^{i(n-n) t} d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} 1 d t=1
$$

In other words, $\left\langle f_{m}: f_{n}\right\rangle=\delta_{m n}$.

## EXERCISES

1. Label the following statements as true or false.
(a) An inner product is a scalar-valued function on the set of ordered pairs of vectors.
(b) An innier product space must be over the field of real or complex numbers.
(c) An inner product is linear in both components.
(d) There is exactly one inner product on the vector space $\mathrm{R}^{n}$.
(e) The triangle inequality only holds in finite-dimensional inner product spaces.
(f) Only square matrices have a conjugate-transpose.
(g) If $x, y$, and $z$ are vectors in an inner product space such that $\langle x, y\rangle=\langle x, z\rangle$, then $y=z$.
(h) If $\langle x, y\rangle=0$ for all $x$ in an imer product space, then $y=0$.
2. Let $x=(2,1+i, i)$ and $y=(2-i, 2,1+2 i)$ be vectors in $\mathrm{C}^{3}$. Compute $\langle x, y\rangle .\|x\|,\|y\|$, and $\|x+y\|$. Then verify both the Cauchy Schwarz inequality and the triangle inecquality.
3. In $\mathrm{C}([0,1])$, let $f(t)=t$ and $g(t)=\boldsymbol{e}^{t}$. Compute $\langle f, g\rangle$ (as defined in Example 3). $\|f\|$, $\|g\|$. and $\|f+g\|$. Then verify both the CauchySchwarz inequality and the triangle inequality.
4. (a) Complete the proof in Example 5 that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ is an inner product (the Frobenius inner product) on $\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)$.
(b) Use the Frobenius inner product to compute $\|A\|,\|B\|$, and $\langle A, B\rangle$ for

$$
A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 2+i \\
3 & i
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad B=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
1+i & 0 \\
i & -i
\end{array}\right)
$$

5. In $\mathrm{C}^{2}$, show that $\langle x: y\rangle=x A y^{*}$ is an inner product, where

$$
A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
1 & i \\
-i & 2
\end{array}\right)
$$

Compute $\langle x, y\rangle$ for $x=(1-i, 2+3 i)$ and $y=(2+i, 3-2 i)$.
6. Complete the proof of Theorem 6.1.
7. Complete the proof of Theorem 6.2.
8. Provide reasons why each of the following is not an immer product on the given vector spaces.
(a) $\langle(a . b),(c . d)\rangle=a c-b d$ on $\mathrm{R}^{2}$.
(b) $\langle A, B\rangle=\operatorname{tr}(A+B)$ on $\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(R)$.
(c) $\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f^{\prime}(t) g(l) d t$ on $\mathrm{P}(R)$, where ' denotes differentiation.
9. Let $\beta$ be a basis for a finite-dimensional inner product space.
(a) Prove that if $\langle x, z\rangle=0$ for all $z \in \beta$, then $x=0$.
(b) Prove that if $\langle x, z\rangle=\langle y, z\rangle$ for all $z \in \beta$. then $x=y$.
10. ${ }^{\dagger}$ Let V be an inner product space, and suppose that $x$ and $y$ are orthogonal vectors in V . Prove that $\|x+y\|^{2}=\|x\|^{2}+\|y\|^{2}$. Deduce the Pythagorean theorem in $\mathrm{R}^{2}$.
11. Prove the parallelogram law on an inner product space $\vee$; that is, show that

$$
\|x+y\|^{2}+\|x-y\|^{2}=2\|x\|^{2}+2\|y\|^{2} \quad \text { for all } x . y \in \mathrm{~V}
$$

What does this equation state about parallelograms in $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ ?
12. ${ }^{\dagger}$ Let $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$ be an orthogonal set in $V$, and let $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k}$ be scalars. Prove that

$$
\left\|\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}\right\|^{2}=\left.\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left|a_{i}\right|\right|^{2}\left\|u_{i}\right\|^{2}
$$

13. Suppose that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{1}$ and $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{2}$ are two immer products on a vector space $V$. Prove that $\langle\cdot, \cdot \cdot\rangle=\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{1}+\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{2}$ is another inner product on $V$.
14. Let $A$ and $B$ be $n \times n$ matrices, and let $c$ be a scalar. Prove that $(A+c B)^{*}=A^{*}+\bar{c} B^{*}$.
15. (a) Prove that if $V$ is an imer product space, then $|\langle x, y\rangle|=\|x\| \cdot\|y\|$ if and only if one of the vectors $x$ or $y$ is a multiple of the other. Hint: If the identity holds and $y \neq 0$, let

$$
a=\frac{\langle x, y\rangle}{\|y\|^{2}}
$$

and let $z=x-a y$. Prove that $y$ and $z$ are orthogonal and

$$
|a|=\frac{\|x\|}{\|y\|} .
$$

Then apply Exercise 10 to $\|x\|^{2}=\|a y+z\|^{2}$ to obtain $\|z\|=0$.
(b) Derive a similar result for the equality $\|x+y\|=\|x\|+\|y\|$, and gencralize it to the case of $n$ vectors.
16. (a) Show thät the vector space $H$ with $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ defined on page 332 is an imeer product space.
(b) Let $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}([0,1])$, and define

$$
\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{1 / 2} f(t) g(t) d t
$$

Is this an imer product on V ?
17. Let $T$ be a lincar operator on an inner product space $V$, and suppose that $\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|$ for all $x$. Prove that T is one-to-one.
18. Let $\vee$ be a vector space over $F$, where $F=R$ or $F=C$, and let W be an inner product space over $F$ with inner product $\langle\cdot \cdot \cdot\rangle$. If $\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}$ is linear, prove that $\langle x, y\rangle^{\prime}=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle$ defines an inner product on $V$ if and only if $T$ is onc-to-one.
19. Let $V$ be an inner product space. Prove that
(a) $\|x \pm y\|^{2}=\|x\|^{2} \pm 2 \Re\langle x, y\rangle+\|y\|^{2}$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$, where $\Re\langle x, y\rangle$ denotes the real part of the complex number $\langle x, y\rangle$.
(b) $|\|x\|-\|y\|| \leq\|x-y\|$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
20. Let $V$ be an imer product space over $F$. Prove the polar identities: For all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$,
(a) $\langle x, y\rangle=\frac{1}{4}\|x+y\|^{2}-\frac{1}{4}\|x-y\|^{2} \quad$ if $F=R$;
(b) $\langle x, y\rangle=\frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=1}^{4} i^{k}\left\|x+i^{k} y\right\|^{2} \quad$ if $F=C$, where $i^{2}=-1$.
21. Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Define

$$
A_{1}=\frac{1}{2}\left(A+A^{*}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad A_{2}=\frac{1}{2 i}\left(A-A^{*}\right)
$$

(a) Prove that $A_{1}^{*}=A_{1}, A_{2}^{*}=\Lambda_{2}$, and $A=A_{1}+i A_{2}$. Would it be reasonable to define $A_{1}$ and $\Lambda_{2}$ to be the real and imaginary parts, respectively, of the matrix $A$ ?
(b) Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Prove that the representation in (a) is unique. That is, prove that if $A=B_{1}+i B_{2}$, where $B_{1}^{*}=B_{1}$ and $B_{2}^{\times}=B_{2}$, then $B_{1}=A_{1}$ and $B_{2}=A_{2}$.
22. Let $V$ be a real or complex vector space (possibly infiuite-dimensional), and let $\beta$ be a basis for $V$. For $x, y \in V$ there exist $v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n} \in \beta$ such that

$$
x=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} y_{i} \quad \text { and } \quad y=\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_{i} v_{i} .
$$

Define

$$
\langle x, y\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} \bar{b}_{i} .
$$

(a) Prove that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ is an imer product on $V$ and that $\beta$ is an orthonormal besis for $V$. Thus cevery real or complex vector space may be regarded as an inner product space.
(b) Prove that if $V=\mathrm{R}^{n}$ or $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{n}$ and $\beta$ is the standard ordered basis, then the inner product defined above is the standard inner product.
23. Let $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{F}^{n}$, and let $\Lambda \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)$.
(a) Prove that $\langle x, A y\rangle=\left\langle A^{*} x, y\right\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(b) Suppose that for some $B \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)$, we have $\langle x, A y\rangle=\langle B x, y\rangle$ for all $x, y \in V$. Prove that $B=A^{*}$.
(c) Let $\alpha$ be the standard ordered basis for $V$. For any orthomormal basis is for $V$, let $Q$ be the $n \times n$ matrix whose columns are the vectors in 3 . Prove that $Q^{*}=Q^{-1}$.
(d) Define lincar opcrators T and U on V by $\mathrm{T}(x)=A x$ and $\mathrm{U}(x)=$ $A^{*} x$. Show that $[\mathrm{U}]_{3}=[T]_{\beta}^{*}$ for any orthonomal basis $\beta$ for V .

The following definition is used in Excreises 2427.
Definition. Let $V$ be a vector space over $F$, where $F$ is cither $R$ or C. Regardless of whether $V$ is or is not an inner product space, we may still define a norm $\|\cdot\|$ as a real-valued function on $V$ satisfying the following threc conditions for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$ and $a \in F$ :
(1) $\|x\| \geq 0$, and $\|x\|=0$ if and only if $x=0$.
(2) $\|a x\|=|a| \cdot\|x\|$.
(3) $\|x+y\| \leq\|r\|+\|y\|$.
24. Prove that the following are noms on the given vector spaces $V$.
(a) $V=\mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F) ; \quad\|A\|=\operatorname{mixim}_{i, j}\left|A_{i j}\right| \quad$ for all $A \in \mathrm{~V}$
(b) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}([0,1]) ; \quad\|f\|=\max _{t \in[0.1]}|f(t)| \quad$ for all $f \in \mathrm{~V}$
(c) $\quad V=\mathrm{C}([0,1]) ; \quad\|f\|=\int_{0}^{1}|f(t)| d t \quad$ for all $f \in \mathrm{~V}$
(d) $\quad V=\mathrm{R}^{2} ; \quad\|(a, b)\|=\max \{|a|,|b|\} \quad$ for all $(a, b) \in \mathrm{V}$
25. Use Excrcise 20 to show that there is no inner product $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ on $R^{2}$ such that $\|x\|^{2}=\langle x, x\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{R}^{2}$ if the norm is defined as in Exercise 24(d).
26. Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on a vector space $V$, and define, for each ordered pair of vectors, the scalar $d(x, y)=\|x-y\|$, called the distance between $x$ and $y$. Prove the following results for all $x, y, z \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(a) $d(x, y) \geq 0$.
(b) $d(x, y)=d(y, x)$.
(c) $d(x, y) \leq d(x, z)+d(z, y)$.
(d) $d(x, x)=0$.
(e) $d(x, y) \neq 0$ if $x \neq y$.
27. Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on a real vector space $V$ satisfying the parallelogram law given in Exercise 11. Define

$$
\langle x, y\rangle=\frac{1}{4}\left[\|x+y\|^{2}-\|x-y\|^{2}\right] .
$$

Prove that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ defines an inner product on V such that $\|x\|^{2}=\langle x, x\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$.

## Hints:

(a) Prove $\langle x, 2 y\rangle=2\langle x, y\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(b) Prove $\langle x+u, y\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle+\langle u, y\rangle$ for all $x, u, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(c) Prove $\langle n x, y\rangle=n\langle x, y\rangle$ for every positive integer $n$ and every $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(d) Prove $m\left\langle\frac{1}{m} x, y\right\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle$ for every positive integer $m$ and every $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(e) Prove $\langle r x, y\rangle=r\langle x, y\rangle$ for every rational number $r$ and every $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(f) Prove $|\langle x, y\rangle| \leq\|x\|\|y\|$ for every $x, y \in V$. Hint: Condition (3) in the definition of norm can be helpful.
(g) Prove that for every $c \in R$, every rational number $r$, and every $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$,

$$
|c\langle x, y\rangle-\langle c x, y\rangle|=|(c-r)\langle x, y\rangle-\langle(c-r) x, y\rangle| \leq 2|c-r|\|x\|\|y\|
$$

(h) Use the fact that for any $c \in R,|c-r|$ can be made arbitrarily small, where $r$ varies over the set of rational numbers, to establish item (b) of the definition of inner product.
28. Let $V$ be a complex inner product space with an inner product $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$. Let $[\cdot, \cdot]$ be the real-valued function such that $[x, y]$ is the real part of the complex number $\langle x, y\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Prove that $[\cdot, \cdot]$ is an inner product for V , where V is regarded as a vector space over $R$. Prove, furthermore, that $[x, i x]=0$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$.
29. Let $V$ be a vector space over $C$, and suppose that $[\cdot, \cdot]$ is a real inner product on $V$, where $V$ is regarded as a vector space over $R$, such that $[x, i x]=0$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$. Let $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ be the complex-valued function defined by

$$
\langle x, y\rangle=[x, y]+i[x, i y] \quad \text { for } x, y \in \mathrm{~V} .
$$

Prove that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ is a complex inner product on $V$.
30. Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm (as defined in Exercise 24) on a complex vector space $V$ satisfying the parallelogram law given in Exercise 11. Prove that there is an inner product $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ on V such that $\|x\|^{2}=\langle x, x\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$.
Hint: Apply Exercise 27 to $V$ regarded as a vector space over $R$. Then apply Exercise 29.

### 6.2 THE GRAM-SCHMIDT ORTHOGONALIZATION PROCESS AND ORTHOGONAL COMPLEMENTS

In previous chapters, we have seen the special role of the standard ordered bases for $C^{n}$ and $\mathrm{R}^{n}$. The special properties of these bases stem from the fact that the basis vectors form an orthonormal set. Just as bases are the building blocks of vector spaces. bases that are also orthonormal sets are the building blocks of imer product spaces. We now name such bases.

Definition. Let V be an inner product space. A subset of V is an orthonormal basis for V if it is an ordered basis that is orthonormal.

## Example 1

The standard ordered basis for $F^{n}$ is an orthonormal basis for $F^{n}$.

## Example 2

The set

$$
\left\{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right),\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\right\}
$$

is an orthonormal basis for $R^{2}$.

The next theorem and its corollaries illustrate why orthonormal sets and. in particular, orthonormal bases are so important.

Theorem 6.3. Let $\vee$ be an inner product space and $S=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$ be an orthogonal subset of $\vee$ consisting of nonzero vectors. If $y \in \operatorname{span}(S)$, then

$$
y=\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}} v_{i}
$$

Proof. Write $y=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}$, where $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k} \in F$. Then, for $1 \leq j \leq k$, we have

$$
\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle=\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i}\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=a_{j}\left\langle v_{j}, v_{j}\right\rangle=a_{j}\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2} .
$$

So $a_{j}=\frac{\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}}$, and the result follows.
The next corollary follows immediately from Theorem 6.3.
Corollary 1. If, in addition to the hypotheses of Theorem 6.3, $S$ is orthonormal and $y \in \operatorname{span}(S)$, then

$$
y=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} .
$$

If $V$ possesses a finite orthonormal basis, then Corollary 1 allows us to compute the coefficients in a linear combination very easily. (Sce Example 3.)

Corollary 2. Let V be an inner product space, and let $S$ be an orthogonal subset of $V$ consisting of nonzero vectors. Then $S$ is lincarly independent.

Proof. Suppose that $v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k} \in S$ and

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}=0
$$

As in the proof of Theorem 6.3 with $y=0$, we have $a_{j}=\left\langle 0, v_{j}\right\rangle /\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}=0$ for all $j$. So $S$ is linearly independent.

## Example 3

By Corollary 2, the orthonormal set

$$
\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1,0), \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,-1,1), \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-1,1,2)\right\}
$$

obtained in Example 8 of Section 6.1 is an orthonormal basis for $\mathrm{R}^{3}$. Let $x=(2,1,3)$. The coefficients given by Corollary 1 to Theorem 6.3 that express $x$ as a linear combination of the basis vectors are

$$
a_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2+1)=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad a_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(2-1+3)=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}},
$$

and

$$
a_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-2+1+6)=\frac{5}{\sqrt{6}} .
$$

As a check, we have

$$
(2.1,3)=\frac{3}{2}(1,1,0)+\frac{4}{3}(1,-1,1)+\frac{5}{6}(-1,1,2) .
$$

Corollary 2 tells us that the vector space $H$ in Section 6.1 contains an infinite linearly independent set, and hence H is not a finite-dimensional vector space.

Of course, we have not yet shown that every finitc-dimensional inner product space possesses an orthonormal basis. The next theorem takes us most of the way in obtaining this result. It tells us how to construct an orthogonal set from a linearly independent set of vectors in such a way that both sets generate the same subspace.

Before stating this theorem, let us consider a simple case. Suppose that $\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}^{\prime}\right\}$ is a linearly independent subset of an inner product space (and hence a basis for some two-dimensional subspace). We want to construct an orthogonal set from $\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}\right\}$ that spans the same subspace. Figure 6.1 suggests that the set $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}$, where $v_{1}=w_{1}$ and $v_{2}=w_{2}-c w_{1}$, has this property if $c$ is chosen so that $v_{2}$ is orthogonal to $W_{1}$.

To find $c$, we need only solve the following equation:

$$
0=\left\langle v_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{2}-c w_{1}, w_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle-c\left\langle w_{1}, w_{1}\right\rangle
$$

So

$$
c=\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|w_{1}\right\|^{2}} .
$$

Thus

$$
v_{2}=w_{2}-\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|w_{1}\right\|^{2}} w_{1}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_02_09_645a3628639374fdb486g-356.jpg?height=269&width=599&top_left_y=93&top_left_x=300)

Figure 6.1

The next theorem shows us that this process can be extended to any finite linearly independent subset.

Theorem 6.4. Let $\vee$ be an innor product space and $S=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}$ be a lincarly independent subset of V . Define $S^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$, where $v_{1}=w_{1}$ and

$$
\begin{equation*}
v_{k}=w_{k}-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}} v_{j} \quad \text { for } 2 \leq k \leq n \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

Then $S^{\prime}$ is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors such that $\operatorname{span}\left(S^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}(S)$.
Proof. The proof is by mathematical induction on $n$, the number of vectors in $S$. For $k=1,2, \ldots, n$, let $S_{k}=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{k}\right\}$. If $n=1$, then the theorem is proved by taking $S_{1}^{\prime}=S_{1}$; i.e., $v_{1}=w_{1} \neq 0$. Assume then that the set $S_{k-1}^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k-1}\right\}$ with the desired properties has been constructed by the repeated use of (1). We show that the set $S_{k}^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k-1}, v_{k}\right\}$ also has the desired propertics, where $v_{k}$ is oltained from $S_{k-1}^{\prime}$ by (1). If $v_{k}=$ 0 . then (1) implies that $u_{k} \in \operatorname{span}\left(S_{k-1}^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k-1}\right)$, which contradicts the assumption that $S_{k}$ is linearly independent. For $1 \leq i \leq k-1$, it follows from (1) that

$$
\left\langle v_{k:}, v_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}}\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle u_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle-\frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}}\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}=0
$$

since $\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle=0$ if $i \neq j$ by the induction assumption that $S_{k-1}^{\prime}$ is orthogonal. Hence $S_{k}^{\prime}$ is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors. Now, by (1), we have that $\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right) \subseteq \operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)$. But by Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.3, $S_{k}^{\prime}$ is linearly independent; so $\operatorname{dim}\left(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right)\right)=\operatorname{dim}\left(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)\right)=k$. Therefore $\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right)=$ $\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)$.

The construction of $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ by the use of Theorem 6.4 is called the Gram-Schmidt process.

## Example 4

In $R^{4}$, let $u_{1}=(1,0,1,0)$. $u_{2}^{\prime}=(1,1,1,1)$, and $u_{3}=(0,1,2,1)$. Then $\left\{w_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}$ is linearly independent. We use the Gram Schmidt process to compute the orthogonal vectors $v_{1}, v_{2}$, and $v_{3}$, and then wo normalize these vectors to obtain an orthonormal set.

Take $v_{1}=w_{1}=(1,0,1,0)$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{2} & =u_{2}-\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, c_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} l_{1} \\
& =(1,1,1,1)-\frac{2}{2}(1,0,1,0) \\
& =(0,1,0,1) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{3} & =w_{3}-\frac{\left\langle w_{3} \cdot v_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} v_{1}-\frac{\left\langle w_{3}, v_{2}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{2}\right\|^{2}} v_{2} \\
& =(0.1,2,1)-\frac{2}{2}(1,0,1.0)-\frac{2}{2}(0,1,0.1) \\
& =(-1,0,1,0) .
\end{aligned}
$$

These vectors can be normalized to obtain the orthonormal basis $\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}$, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u_{1}=\frac{1}{\left\|r_{1}\right\|} v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,0,1,0), \\
& u_{2}=\frac{1}{\left\|r_{2}\right\|} v_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(0,1,0,1) .
\end{aligned}
$$

and

$$
u_{3}=\frac{v_{3}}{\left\|v_{3}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1.0,1,0)
$$

## Example 5

Let $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}(R)$ with the immer product $\langle f(x): g(x)\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t$, and consider the subspace $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$ with the standard ordered basis 3 . We use the Gram-Schmidt process to replace $\beta$ by an orthogonal basis $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}$ for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$, and then use this orthogonal basis to obtain an orthonormal basis for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$.

Take $r_{1}=1$. Then $\left\|r_{1}\right\|^{2}=\int_{-1}^{1} 1^{2} d t=2$, and $\left\langle r, r_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t \cdot 1 d t=0$. Thus

$$
v_{2}=x-\frac{\left\langle y_{1}, x\right\rangle}{\left\|c_{1}\right\|^{2}}=x-\frac{0}{2}=x
$$

Furthermore,

$$
\left\langle x^{2}, v_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} \cdot 1 d t=\frac{2}{3} \quad \text { and } \quad\left\langle x^{2}, v_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} \cdot t d t=0 .
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{3} & =x^{2}-\frac{\left\langle x^{2}, v_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} v_{1}-\frac{\left\langle x^{2}, v_{2}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{2}\right\|^{2}} v_{2} \\
& =x^{2}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1-0 \cdot x \\
& =x^{2}-\frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

We conclude that $\left\{1, x, x^{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right\}$ is an orthogonal basis for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$.
To obtain an orthonormal basis, we normalize $v_{1}, v_{2}$, and $v_{3}$ to obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\int_{-1}^{1} 1^{2} d t}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
& u_{2}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} d t}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x
\end{aligned}
$$

and similarly,

$$
u_{3}=\frac{v_{3}}{\left\|v_{3}\right\|}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right)
$$

Thus $\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}$ is the desired orthonormal basis for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$.
If we continue applying the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process to the basis $\left\{1, x, x^{2}, \ldots\right\}$ for $\mathrm{P}(R)$, we obtain an orthogonal basis whose elements are called the Legendre polynomials. The orthogonal polynomials $v_{1}, v_{2}$, and $v_{3}$ in Example 5 are the first three Legendre polynomials.

The following result gives us a simple method of representing a vector as a lincar combination of the vectors in an orthonormal basis.

Theorem 6.5. Let $V$ be a nonzero finite-dimensional inncr product space. Then $V$ has an orthonormal basis $\beta$. Furthermorc, if $\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ and $x \in \mathrm{~V}$, then

$$
x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} .
$$

Proof. Let $\beta_{0}$ be an ordered basis for V. Apply Theorem 6.4 to obtain an orthogonal set $\beta^{\prime}$ of nonzero vectors with $\operatorname{span}\left(\beta^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}\left(\beta_{0}\right)=\mathrm{V}$. By normalizing cach vector in $\beta^{\prime}$, we obtain an orthonormal set $\beta$ that generates V. By Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.3, $\beta$ is linearly independent; therefore $\beta$ is an orthonormal basis for V . The remainder of the theorem follows from Corollary 1 to Theorem 6.3.

## Example 6

We use Theorem 6.5 to represent the polynomial $f(x)=1+2 x+3 x^{2}$ as a linear combination of the vectors in the orthonormal basis $\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}$ for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$ obtained in Example 5 . Observe that

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=2 \sqrt{2} \\
& \left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} t\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

and

$$
\left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 t^{2}-1\right)\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{2 \sqrt{10}}{5} .
$$

Therefore $f(x)=2 \sqrt{2} u_{1}+\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{3} u_{2}+\frac{2 \sqrt{10}}{5} u_{3}$.
Theorem 6.5 gives us a simple method for computing the entries of the matrix representation of a linear operator with respect to an orthonormal basis.

Corollary. Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional inner product space with an orthonormal basis $\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$. Let $T$ be a linear operator on V , and let $A=[\mathrm{T}]_{; j}$. Then for any $i$ and $j, A_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle$.

Proof. From Theorem 6.5, we have

$$
\mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i}
$$

Hence $A_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle$.
The scalars $\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle$ given in Theorem 6.5 have been studied extensively for special inner product spaces. Although the vectors $v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}$ were chosen from an orthonormal basis, we introduce a terminology associated with orthonormal sets $\beta$ in more general inner product spaces.

Definition. Let $\beta$ be an orthonormal subset (possibly infinite) of an inner product space V , and let $x \in \mathrm{~V}$. We define the Fourier coefficients of $x$ relative to $\beta$ to be the scalars $\langle x, y\rangle$, wherc $y \in \beta$.

In the first half of the 19th century, the French mathematician Jean Baptiste Fourier was associated with the study of the scalars

$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) \sin n t d t \text { and } \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) \cos n t d t
$$

or more generally,

$$
c_{n}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) e^{-i n t} d t
$$

for a function $f$. In the context of Example 9 of Section 6.1, we see that $c_{n}=\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle$, where $f_{n}(t)=e^{i n t}$; that is, $c_{n}$ is the $n$th Fourier coefficient for a continuous function $f \in \mathrm{~V}$ relative to $S$. These coefficients are the "classical" Fourier cocfficients of a function, and the literature concerning the behavior of these coefficients is extensive. We learn more about these Fourier coefficients in the remainder of this chapter.

## Example 7

Let $S=\left\{e^{\text {int }}: n\right.$ is an integer $\}$. In Example 9 of Section 6.1, $S$ was shown to be an orthonormal set in H . We compute the Fouricr coefficients of $f(t)=t$ relative to $S$. Using integration by parts, we have, for $n \neq 0$,

$$
\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \overline{t e^{i n t}} d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} t e^{-i n t} d t=\frac{-1}{i n}
$$

and, for $n=0$,

$$
\langle f, 1\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} t(1) d t=\pi
$$

As a result of these computations, and using Exercise 16 of this section, we obtain an upper bound for the sum of a special infinite serics as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|f\|^{2} & \geq \sum_{n=-k}^{-1}\left|\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2}+|\langle f, 1\rangle|^{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{k}\left|\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2} \\
& =\sum_{n=-k}^{-1} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \\
& =2 \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

for every $k$. Now, using the fact that $\|f\|^{2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi^{2}$, we obtain

$$
\frac{4}{3} \pi^{2} \geq 2 \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2}
$$

or

$$
\frac{\pi^{2}}{6} \geq \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}}
$$

Because this inequality holds for all $k$, we may let $k \rightarrow \infty$ to obtain

$$
\frac{\pi^{2}}{6} \geq \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2}}
$$

Additional results may be produced by replacing $f$ by other functions.
We are now ready to proceed with the concept of an orthogonal complement.

Definition. Let $S$ be a noncinpty subset of an inner product space V . We define $S^{\perp}$ (read " $S$ perp") to be the set of all vectors in $V$ that are orthogonal to every vector in $S$; that is, $S^{\perp}=\{x \in \mathrm{~V}:\langle x, y\rangle=0$ for all $y \in S\}$. The set $S^{\perp}$ is called the orthogonal complement of $S$.

It is easily seen that, $S^{\perp}$ is a subspace of V for any subset $S$ of V .

## Example 8

The reader should verify that $\{0\}^{\perp}=V$ and $V^{\perp}=\{0\}$ for any inner product space $V$.

## Example 9

If $V=\mathrm{R}^{3}$ and $S=\left\{e_{3}\right\}$, then $S^{\perp}$ equals the $x y$-plane (see Exercise 5).
Exercise 18 provides an interesting example of an orthogonal complement in an infinite-dimensional inner product space.

Consider the problem in $\mathrm{R}^{3}$ of finding the distance from a point $P$ to a plane W. (See Figure 6.2.) Problems of this type arise in many settings. If we let $y$ be the vector determined by 0 and $P$, we may restate the problem as follows: Determine the vector $u$ in $W$ that is "closest" to $y$. The desired distance is clearly given by $\|y-u\|$. Notice from the figure that the vector $z=y-u$ is orthogonal to every vector in W , and so $z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$.

The next result presents a practical method of finding $u$ in the case that W is a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_02_09_645a3628639374fdb486g-362.jpg?height=509&width=816&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=270)

Figure 6.2

Theorem 6.6. Let $W$ be a finitc-dimensional subspace of an inner product space V , and let $y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Then there exist unique vectors $u \in \mathrm{~W}$ and $z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$ such that $y=u+z$. Furthermore, if $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$ is an orthonormal basis for W , then

$$
u=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i}
$$

Proof. Let $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$ be an orthonormal basis for W , let $u$ be as defined in the preceding equation, and let $z=y-u$. Clearly $u \in \mathrm{~W}$ and $y=u+z$.

To show that $z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$, it suffices to show, by Exercise 7, that $z$ is orthogonal to each $v_{j}$. For any $j$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle z, v_{j}\right\rangle & =\left\langle\left(y-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle=\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle \\
& =\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle-\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle=0
\end{aligned}
$$

To show uniqueness of $u$ and $z$, suppose that $y=u+z=u^{\prime}+z^{\prime}$, where $u^{\prime} \in \mathrm{W}$ and $z^{\prime} \in \mathrm{W}^{\perp}$. Then $u-u^{\prime}=z^{\prime}-z \in \mathrm{~W} \cap \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}=\{0\}$. Therefore. $u=u^{\prime}$ and $z=z^{\prime}$.

Corollary. In the notation of Theorm 6.6. the vector $u$ is the unique vector in W that is "closest" to $y$; that is, for any $x \in \mathrm{~W},\|y-x\| \geq\|y-u\|$. and this inequality is an equality if and only if $x=u$.

Proof. As in Theorem 6.6, we have that $y=u+z$, where $z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$. Let $x \in \mathrm{~W}$. Then $u-x$ is orthogonal to $z$, so, by Exercise 10 of Section 6.1 , we
have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|y-x\|^{2} & =\|u+z-x\|^{2}=\|(u-x)+z\|^{2}=\|u-x\|^{2}+\|z\|^{2} \\
& \geq\|z\|^{2}=\|y-u\|^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now suppose that $\|y-x\|=\|y-u\|$. Then the inequality above becomes an equality, and therefore $\|u-x\|^{2}+\|z\|^{2}=\|z\|^{2}$. It follows that $\|u-x\|=0$, and hence $x=u$. The proof of the converse is obvious.

The vector $u$ in the corollary is called the orthogonal projection of $y$ on W. We will see the importance of orthogonal projections of vectors in the application to least squares in Section 6.3.

## Example 10

Let $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{3}(R)$ with the inner product

$$
\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t \quad \text { for all } f(x), g(x) \in \mathrm{V}
$$

We compute the orthogonal projection $f_{1}(x)$ of $f(x)=x^{3}$ on $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$.
By Example 5,

$$
\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}=\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x, \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right)\right\}
$$

is an orthonormal basis for $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$. For these vectors, we have

$$
\left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} d t=0, \quad\left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} t d t=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{5}
$$

and

$$
\left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 t^{2}-1\right) d t=0
$$

Hence

$$
f_{1}(x)=\left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle u_{1}+\left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle u_{2}+\left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle u_{3}=\frac{3}{5} x .
$$

It was shown (Corollary 2 to the replacement theorem, p. 47) that any linearly independent set in a finite-dimensional vector space can be extended to a basis. The next theorem provides an interesting analog for an orthonormal subset of a finite-dimensional inner product space.

Theorem 6.7. Suppose that $S=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$ is an orthonormal set in an $n$-dimensional inner product space $V$. Then
(a) $S$ can be extendcd to an orthonormal basis $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}, v_{k+1}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ for V .
(b) If $\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}(S)$, then $S_{1}=\left\{v_{k+1}, v_{k+2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ is an orthonormal basis for $W^{\perp}$ (using the preceding notation).
(c) If $W$ is any subspace of $V$, then $\operatorname{dim}(V)=\operatorname{dim}(W)+\operatorname{dim}\left(W^{\perp}\right)$.

Proof. (a) By Corollary 2 to the replacement theorem (p. 47), $S$ can be extended to an ordered basis $S^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}, w_{k+1}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}$ for $V$. Now apply the Gram-Schmidt process to $S^{\prime}$. The first $k$ vectors resulting from this process are the vectors in $S$ by Exercise 8, and this new set spans V. Normalizing the last $n-k$ vectors of this set produces an orthonormal set that spans $V$. The result now follows.
(b) Because $S_{1}$ is a subset of a basis, it is linearly independent. Since $S_{1}$ is clearly a subset of $\mathrm{W}^{\perp}$, we need only show that it spans $\mathrm{W}^{\perp}$. Note that, for any $x \in \mathrm{~V}$, we have

$$
x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} .
$$

If $x \in W_{-}^{\perp}$, then $\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle=0$ for $1 \leq i \leq k$. Therefore,

$$
x=\sum_{i=k+1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} \in \operatorname{span}\left(S_{1}\right) .
$$

(c) Let W be a subspace of V . It is a finite-dimensional inner product space because V is, and so it has an orthonormal basis $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}$. By (a) and (b), we have

$$
\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{V})=n=k+(n-k)=\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{W})+\operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\right)
$$

## Example 11

Let $W=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}\right\}\right)$ in $F^{3}$. Then $x=(a, b, c) \in W^{\perp}$ if and only if $0=$ $\left\langle x, e_{1}\right\rangle=a$ and $0=\left\langle x, e_{2}\right\rangle=b$. So $x=(0,0, c)$, and therefore $\mathrm{W}^{\perp}=$ span $\left(\left\{e_{3}\right\}\right)$. One can deduce the same result by noting that $e_{3} \in W^{\perp}$ and, from (c), that $\operatorname{dim}\left(W^{\perp}\right)=3-2=1$.

## EXERCISES

1. Label the following statements as true or false.
(a) The Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process allows us to construct an orthonormal set from an arbitrary set of vectors.
(b) Every nonzero finite-dimensional inner product space has an orthonormal basis.
(c) The orthogonal complement of any set is a sulbspace.
(d) If $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ is a basis for an inner product space V , then for any $x \in \mathrm{~V}$ the scalars $\left\langle x^{\prime}, d_{i}\right\rangle$ are the Fourier coefficients of $x$.
(e) An orthonormal basis must be an ordered basis.
(f) Every orthogonal set is linearly indepondent.
(g) Every orthonormal set is linearly independent.
2. In each part, apply the Gram Schmidt process to the given subset $S$ of the inner product space V to obtain an orthogonal basis for $\operatorname{span}(S)$. Then normalize the vectors in this basis to obtain an orthonormal basis ; 3 for $\operatorname{span}(S)$, and compute the Fourier coeflicients of the given vector relative to 3 . Finally. use Theorem 6.5 to verify your result.
(a) $V=R^{3} . S=\{(1,0,1),(0.1,1),(1,3.3)\}$, and $: r=(1,1,2)$
(b) $V=R^{3} . S=\{(1,1,1),(0.1,1),(0.0,1)\}$, and $: x=(1,0,1)$
(c) $V=\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$ with the imner product $\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t$, $S=\left\{1, x, x^{2}\right\}$, and $h(x)=1+x$
(d) $V=\operatorname{span}(S)$, where $S=\{(1, i, 0),(1-i, 2,4 i)\}$, and $r=(3+i .4 i,--1)$
(e) $V=\mathrm{R}^{\cdot}, S=\{(2,-1,-2,4),(-2.1 .-5,5),(-1,3,7,11)\}$. and $x=$ (-11.8. -4, 18)
(f) $\quad \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{1} . S=\{(1,-2,-1,3) .(3,6,3,-1),(1,4,2,8)\}$, and $x=(-1,2,1,1)$
(g) $V=M_{2 \times 2}(R), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{rr}3 & 5 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right),\left(\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 9 \\ 5 & -1\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{rr}7 & -17 \\ 2 & -6\end{array}\right)\right\}$, and $A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 27 \\ -4 & 8\end{array}\right)$
(h) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2 \times 2}(R), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{rr}11 & 1 \\ 2 & 5\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{ll}4 & -12 \\ 3 & -16\end{array}\right)\right\}$. and $A=$ $\left(\begin{array}{rr}8 & 6 \\ 25 & -13\end{array}\right)$
(i) $V=\operatorname{span}(S)$ with the inmer product $\langle f \cdot g\rangle=\int_{0}^{\pi} f(t) g(t) d t$, $S=\{\sin t, \cos t, 1, t\}$, and $h(t)=2 t+1$
(j) $V=C^{4} . S=\{(1, i, 2-i,-1),(2+3 i .3 i, 1-i, 2 i)$, $(-1+7 i .6+10 i, 11-4 i, 3+4 i)\}$, and $x=(-2+7 i .6+9 i .9-3 i, 4+4 i)$
(k) $V=\mathrm{C}^{4}, S=\{(-4,3-2 i, i, 1-4 i)$, $(-1-5 i, 5-4 i,-3+5 i, 7-2 i) \cdot(-27-i,-7-6 i .-15+25 i,-7-6 i)\}$, and $x=(-13-7 i,-12+3 i,-39-11 i,-26+3 i)$
(l) $V=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(C): S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{cc}1-i & -2-3 i \\ 2+2 i & 4+i\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{cc}8 i & 4 \\ -3-3 i & -4+4 i\end{array}\right)\right.$.

$$
\left.\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-25-38 i & -2-13 i \\
12-78 i & -7+24 i
\end{array}\right)\right\} . \text { and } A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-2+8 i & -13+i \\
10-10 i & 9-9 i
\end{array}\right)
$$

(m) $V=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(C), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1+i & -i \\ 2-i & 1+3 i\end{array}\right),\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1-7 i & -3-8 i \\ 1+10 i & -6-2 i\end{array}\right)\right.$,

$$
\left.\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-11-132 i & -34-31 i \\
7-126 i & -71-5 i
\end{array}\right)\right\}, \text { and } A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-7+5 i & 3+18 i \\
9-6 i & -3+7 i
\end{array}\right)
$$

3. In $R^{2}$, let

$$
\beta=\left\{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right),\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\} .
$$

Find the Fourier coefficients of (3.4) relative to is.
4. Let $S=\{(1,(), i),(1.2,1)\}$ in $C^{3}$. Compute $S^{\perp}$.
5. Let $S_{0}=\left\{r_{0}\right\}$, where $x_{0}$ is a nonzero vector in $R^{3}$. Describe $S_{0}^{\perp}$ geonetrically. Now suppose that $S=\left\{x_{1}, x_{2}\right\}$ is a lincarly independent subset of $\mathrm{R}^{3}$. Describe $S^{\perp}$ geometrically.
6. Let V be an imer product space, and let W be a finite-dimensional subspace of V . If $x \notin \mathrm{~W}$, prove that there exists $y \in \mathrm{~V}$ such that $y \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$, but $\langle r, y\rangle \neq 0$. Hint: Use Theorem 6.(i.
7. Let $\beta$ be a basis for a subspace $W$ of an imer product space $V$. and let $z \in V$. Prove that $z \in W^{\perp}$ if and only if $\left\langle z, v^{\prime}\right\rangle=0$ for every $u^{\prime} \in \beta$.
8. Prove that if $\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}\right\}$ is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors, then the vectors $r_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}$ derived from the Gram Schmidt process satisfy $r_{i}=u_{i}$ for $i=1,2 \ldots, n$. Hint: Use mathematical induction.
9. Let $W=\operatorname{span}(\{(i, 0,1)\})$ in $C^{3}$. Find orthonormal bases for $W$ and $W^{\perp}$.
10. Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an imer product space $V$. Prove that there exists a projection $T$ on $W$ along $W^{\perp}$ that satisfies $\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})=\mathrm{W}^{\perp}$. In addition. prove that $\|\mathrm{T}(x)\| \leq\|x\|$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$. Hint: Use Theorem 6.6 and Exercise 10 of Section 6.1. (Projections are defined in the exercises of Section 2.1.)
11. Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix with complex entrics. Prove that $A A^{*}=I$ if and only if the rows of $A$ form an orthonormal basis for $\mathrm{C}^{n}$.
12. Prove that for any matrix $A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F),\left(\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right)\right)^{\perp}=\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)$.
13. Let V be an inner product space, $S$ and $S_{0}$ be subscts of V , and W be a finite-dimensional subspace of V . Prove the following results.
(a) $S_{0} \subseteq S$ implies that $S^{\perp} \subseteq S_{0}^{\perp}$.
(b) $S \subseteq\left(S^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}$; so span $(S) \subseteq\left(S^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}$.
(c) $W=\left(W^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}$. IIint: Use Exercise 6.
(d) $V=W \ominus W^{\perp}$. (See the exercises of Section 1.3.)
14. Let $W_{1}$ and $W_{2}$ be subspaces of a finite-dimensional inner product space. Prove that $\left(W_{1}+W_{2}\right)^{\perp}=W_{1}^{\perp} \cap W_{2}^{\perp}$ and $\left(W_{1} \cap W_{2}\right)^{\perp}=W_{1}^{\perp}+W_{2}^{\perp}$. (See the definition of the sum of subsets of a vector space on page 22.) Hint for the second equation: Apply Excrcise 13(c) to the first equation.
15. Let $V$ be a finitc-dimensional inner product space over $F$.
(a) Parseval's Identity. Let $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ be an orthonormal basis for V . For any $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$ prove that

$$
\langle x, y\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle \overline{\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle} .
$$

(b) Use (a) to prove that if $\beta$ is an orthonormal basis for $V$ with inner product $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$, then for any $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$

$$
\left\langle\phi_{\beta}(x), \phi_{\beta}(y)\right\rangle^{\prime}=\left\langle[x]_{\beta},[y]_{\beta}\right\rangle^{\prime}=\langle x, y\rangle,
$$

where $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle^{\prime}$ is the standard inner product on $\mathrm{F}^{n}$.
16. (a) Bessel's Incquality. Let V be an inner product spacc, and let $S=$ $\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots v_{n}\right\}$ be an orthonormal subset of $V$. Prove that for any $x \in \mathrm{~V}$ we have

$$
\|x\|^{2} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle\right|^{2}
$$

Iint: Apply Theorem 6.6 to $x \in \mathrm{~V}$ and $\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}(S)$. Then use Exercise 10 of Section 6.1.
(b) In the context of (a), prove that Bessel's inequality is an equality if and only if $x \in \operatorname{span}(S)$.
17. Let T be a linear operator on an inner product space V . If $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=0$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$, prove that $\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}_{0}$. In fact, prove this result if the equality holds for all $x$ and $y$ in some basis for V .
18. Let $V=C([-1,1])$. Suppose that $W_{e}$ and $W_{o}$ denote the subspaces of $V$ consisting of the even and odd functions, respectively. (Sec Exercise 22
of Section 1.3.) Prove that $\mathrm{W}_{e}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{o}$, where the inner product on V is defined by

$$
\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t
$$

19. In each of the following parts, find the orthogonal projection of the given vector on the given subspace $W$ of the inner product space $V$.
(a) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{2}, u=(2,6)$, and $\mathrm{W}=\{(\dot{x}, y): y=4 x\}$.
(b) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{3}, u=(2,1,3)$, and $\mathrm{W}=\{(x, y, z): x+3 y-2 z=0\}$.
(c) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}(R)$ with the inner product $\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t$, $h(x)=4+3 x-2 x^{2}$, and $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)$.
20. In each part of Exercise 19, find the distance from the given vector to the subspace $W$.
21. Let $V=\mathrm{C}([-1,1]\rangle$ with the inner product $\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t$, and let $W$ be the subspace $\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$, viewed as a space of functions. Use the orthonormal basis obtained in Example 5 to compute the "best" (closest) second-degree polynomial approximation of the function $h(t)=$ $e^{t}$ on the interval $[-1,1]$.
22. Let $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}([0,1])$ with the inner product $\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t$. Let W be the subspace spanned by the linearly independent set $\{t, \sqrt{t}\}$.
(a) Find an orthonormal basis for W .
(b) Let $h(t)=t^{2}$. Use the orthonormal basis obtained in (a) to obtain the "best" (closest) approximation of $h$ in W.
23. Let V be the vector space defined in Example 5 of Section 1.2, the space of all sequences $\sigma$ in $F$ (where $F=R$ or $F=C$ ) such that $\sigma(n) \neq 0$ for only finitely many positive integers $n$. For $\sigma, \mu \in \mathrm{V}$, we define $\langle\sigma, \mu\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sigma(n) \overline{\mu(n)}$. Since all but a finite number of terms of the series are zero, the series converges.
(a) Prove that $\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle$ is an inner product on $V$, and hence $V$ is an inner product space.
(b) For each positive integer $n$, let $e_{n}$ be the sequence defined by $e_{n}(k)=\delta_{n, k}$, where $\delta_{n, k}$ is the Kronecker delta. Prove that $\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots\right\}$ is an orthonormal basis for $V$.
(c) Let $\sigma_{n}=e_{1}+e_{n}$ and $\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{\sigma_{n}: n \geq 2\right\}\right.$.
(i) Prove that $e_{1} \notin \mathrm{~W}$, so $\mathrm{W} \neq \mathrm{V}$.
(ii) Prove that $W^{\perp}=\{0\}$, and conclude that $W \neq\left(W^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}$.

Thus the assumption in Exercise 13(c) that W is finite-dimensional is essential.

### 6.3 THE ADJOINT OF A LINEAR OPERATOR

In Section 6.1, we defined the conjugate transpose $A^{*}$ of a matrix $A$. For a lincar operator $T$ on an inner product space $V$, we now define a related lincar operator on V called the adjoint of T , whose matrix representation with respect to any orthonormal basis $\beta$ for V is $[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{*}$. The analogy between conjugation of complex mumbers and adjoints of linear operators will become apparent. We first need a preliminary result.

Lct V be an inner product space, and let $y \in \mathrm{~V}$. The function $\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F$ defined by $\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle$ is clearly linear. More interesting is the fact that if V is finite-dimensional, every linear transformation from V into $F$ is of this form.

Theorem 6.8. Let $\vee$ bc a finite-dimensional inner product space over $F$, and let $\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F$ be a linear transformation. Then there exists a unique vector $y \in \mathrm{~V}$ such that $\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$.

Proof. Let $\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$ be an orthonormal basis for V , and let

$$
y=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{\mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)} v_{i}
$$

Define $\mathrm{h}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F$ by $\mathrm{h}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle$, which is clearly lincar. Furthermore, for: $1 \leq j \leq n$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{h}\left(v_{j}\right)=\left\langle v_{j}, y\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{j}, \sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{\mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)} v_{i}\right\rangle & =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle \\
& =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right) \delta_{j i}=\mathrm{g}\left(v_{j}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Since g and h both agree on $\beta$, we have that $\mathrm{g}=\mathrm{h}$ by the corollary to Theorem 2.6 (p. 73).

To show that $y$ is unique, suppose that $\mathrm{g}(x)=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle$ for all $x$. Then $\langle x, y\rangle=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle$ for all $x$; so by Theorem 6.1(e) (p. 333), we have $y=y^{\prime}$.

## Example 1

Define $\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow R$ by $\mathrm{g}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=2 a_{1}+a_{2}$; clearly g is a linear transformation. Let $\beta=\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}\right\}$, and let $y=\mathrm{g}\left(e_{1}\right) e_{1}+\mathrm{g}\left(e_{2}\right) e_{2}=2 e_{1}+e_{2}=(2,1)$, as in the proof of Theorem 6.8. Then $g\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=\left\langle\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right),(2,1)\right\rangle=2 a_{1}+a_{2}$.

Theorem 6.9. Let V be a finite-dimensional inner product space, and let T be a linear operator on V . Then there exists a mique function $\mathrm{T}^{*}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}$ such that $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Furthermore. $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ is linear.

Proof. Let $y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Define $\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F$ by $\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$. We first show that g is lincar. Let $x_{1}, x_{2} \in \mathrm{~V}$ and $c \in F$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{g}\left(c x_{1}+x_{2}\right) & =\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(c x_{1}+x_{2}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle \\
& =r\left\langle\mathrm{~T}\left(x_{1}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle+\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2}\right), y\right\rangle=\operatorname{cg}\left(x_{1}\right)+\mathrm{g}\left(x_{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $g$ is linear.
We now apply Theorem 6.8 to obtain a unique vector $y^{\prime} \in \mathrm{V}$ such that $\mathrm{g}(x)=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle$; that is. $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle$ for all $r \in \mathrm{~V}$. Defining $\mathrm{T}^{*}: \mathrm{V} \longrightarrow \mathrm{V}$ by $\mathrm{T}^{*}(y)=y^{\prime}$, we have $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle$.

To show that $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ is lincar, let $y_{1}, y_{2} \in \mathrm{~V}$ and $c \in F$. Then for any $x \in \mathrm{~V}$. we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}\left(c y_{1}+y_{2}\right)\right\rangle & =\left\langle\mathrm{T}(. r), c y_{1}+y_{2}\right\rangle \\
& =\bar{c}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}(r), y_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle\mathrm{T}(\cdot r), y_{2}\right\rangle \\
& =\bar{c}\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)\right\rangle+\left\langle r, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)\right\rangle \\
& =\left\langle x \cdot c \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)\right\rangle .
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $x$ is arbitrary: $\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(r y_{1}+y_{2}\right)=c \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)$ by Theorem 6.1(e) (p. 333).

Finally, we need to show that $T^{*}$ is unique. Suppose that $U: V \rightarrow V$ is linear and that it satisfics $\langle\mathrm{T}(x) . y\rangle=\langle r, \mathrm{U}(y)\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Then $\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle=\langle x, \mathrm{U}(y)\rangle$ for all $r, y \in \mathrm{~V}$, so $\mathrm{T}^{*}=\mathrm{U}$.

The linear operator $T^{*}$ deseribed in Theorem 6.9 is called the adjoint of the operator $T$. The symbol $T$ " is read " $T$ star."

Thus $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ is the mique operator on V satisfying $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle r, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle$ for all $. r, y \in \mathrm{~V}$. Note that we also have

$$
\langle r, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\overline{\langle\mathrm{T}(y), x\rangle}=\overline{\left\langle y, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(x)\right\rangle}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(. x), y\right\rangle ;
$$

so $\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x), y\right\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$. We may view these equations symbolically as adding a $*$ to $T$ when shifting its position inside the inner product symbol.

For an infinite-dimensional inner product space, the adjoint of a lincar operator T may be defined to be the function $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ such that $\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle$ for all $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$, provided it exists. Although the uniqueness and linearity of $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ follow as before. the existence of the adjoint is not guaranteed (see Exercise 2.4). The reader should ohserve the necessity of the hypothesis of finitedimonsionality in the proof of Theorem 6.8. Many of the theorems we prove
about adjoints, nevertheless, do not depend on $V$ being finite-dimensional. Thus, unless stated otherwiss, for the remainuler of this chapter we adopl the convention that a reference to the adjoint of a linear operator on an infinitedimensional inner product spare assumes its pxistence.

Theorem 6.10 is a useful result for computing aljoints.
Theorem 6.10. Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional inner product space. and let 3 be an orthonormal hasis for V . If T is a linear operator on V , then

$$
\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{B}=[\mathrm{T}]_{i j}^{*} .
$$

Proof. Let $A=[T]_{i \beta}, B=\left[T^{*}\right]_{3 i}$, and $; 3=\left\{v_{1}, r_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}$. Then from the corollary to Theorem 6.5) (p. 346), we have

$$
B_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle=\overline{\left\langle v_{i}, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle}=\overline{\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle}=\overline{\Lambda_{j i}}=\left(A^{*}\right)_{i j}
$$

Hence $B=A^{*}$.
Corollary. Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. Then $L_{A^{-}}=\left(L_{A}\right)^{*}$.
Proof. If 3 is the standard ordered basis for $\mathrm{F}^{\prime \prime}$, then, by Theorem $2.1 \%$ (p. 93), we have $\left[\mathrm{L}_{A}\right]_{3}=A$. Hence $\left[\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}\right]_{; 3}=\left[\mathrm{L}_{A}\right]_{;}^{*}=A^{*}=\left[\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right]_{3}$. and so $\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}$.

As an illustration of Theorem ( 6.10 , we compute the arljoint of arspecific: linear operator.

## Example 2

Let T be the linear operator on $\mathrm{C}^{-2}$ defined by $\mathrm{T}\left(a_{1} \cdot a_{2}\right)=\left(2 i a_{1}+3 a_{2}, a_{1}-a_{2}\right)$. If 3 is the standard ordered basis for $C^{2}$. them

$$
[T]_{j \beta}=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
2 i & 3 \\
1 & -1
\end{array}\right) .
$$

So

$$
\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{i}=[\mathrm{T}]_{i}^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
-2 i & 1 \\
3 & -1
\end{array}\right) .
$$

Hence

$$
\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=\left(-2 i a_{1}+a_{2}, 3 a_{1}-a_{2}\right)
$$

The following theorem suggests an analogy between the conjugates of complex numbers and the adjoints of linear operators.

Theorem 6.11. Let V be an inner product space, and let T and U be linear operators on V . Then
(a) $(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}$;
(b) $(c \mathrm{~T})^{*}=\bar{c} \mathrm{~T}^{*}$ for any $c \in F$;
(c) $(\mathrm{TU})^{*}=\mathrm{U}^{*} \mathrm{~T}^{*}$;
(d) $\mathrm{T}^{* *}=\mathrm{T}$;
(e) $I^{*}=I$.

Proof. We prove (a) and (d); the rest are proved similarly. Let $x, y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(a) Because

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle x,(\mathrm{~T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}(y)\right\rangle & =\langle(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})(x), y\rangle=\langle\mathrm{T}(x)+\mathrm{U}(x), y\rangle \\
& =\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle+\langle\mathrm{U}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle+\left\langle x, \mathrm{U}^{*}(y)\right\rangle \\
& =\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)+\mathrm{U}^{*}(y)\right\rangle=\left\langle x,\left(\mathrm{~T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}\right)(y)\right\rangle .
\end{aligned}
$$

$\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}$ has the property unique to $(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}$. Hence $\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}=(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}$.
(d) Similarly, since

$$
\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x) \cdot y\right\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{* *}(y)\right\rangle,
$$

(d) follows.

The same proof works in the infinite-dimensional case, provided that the existence of $\mathrm{T}^{*}$ and $\mathrm{U}^{*}$ is assumed.

Coróllary. Let $A$ and $B$ be $n \times n$ matrices. Then
(a) $(A+B)^{*}=A^{*}+B^{*}$;
(b) $(c A)^{*}=\bar{c} A^{*}$ for all $c \in F$;
(c) $(A B)^{*}=B^{*} A^{*}$;
(d) $A^{* *}=A$;
(c) $I^{*}=I$.

Proof. We prove only (c): the remaining parts can be proved similarly.
Since $\mathrm{L}_{(A B)^{*}}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{A B}\right)^{*}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{A} \mathrm{~L}_{B}\right)^{*}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{B}\right)^{*}\left(\mathrm{~L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{B^{*}} \mathrm{~L}_{A^{*}}=\mathrm{L}_{B^{*} A^{*}}$, we have $(A B)^{*}=B^{*} A^{*}$.

In the preceding proof, we relied on the corollary to Theorem 6.10. An alternative proof, which holds even for nonsquare matrices, can be given by appealing directly to the definition of the conjugate transpose of a matrix (see Excrcise 5).

## Least Squares Approximation

Consider the following problem: An experimenter collects data by taking measurencuts $y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{m}$ at times $t_{1}, t_{2}, \ldots, t_{m}$, respectively. For example, he or she may be measuring unemployment at various times during some period. Suppose that the data $\left(t_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left\langle t_{2}, y_{2}\right), \ldots,\left(t_{m}, y_{m}\right)$ are plotted as points in the plane. (See Figure 6.3.) From this plot, the experimenter
feels that there exists an essentially linear relationship between $y$ and $t$, say $y=c t+d$, and would like to find the constants $c$ and $d$ so that the line $y=c t+d$ represents the best possible fit to the data collected. One such estimate of fit is to calculate the error $E$ that represents the sum of the squares of the vertical distances from the points to the line; that is,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_02_09_645a3628639374fdb486g-373.jpg?height=819&width=932&top_left_y=337&top_left_x=230)

Figure 6.3
Thus the problem is reduced to finding the constants $c$ and $d$ that minimize $E$. (For this reason the line $y=c t+d$ is called the least squares line.) If we let

$$
A=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
t_{1} & 1 \\
t_{2} & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
t_{m} & 1
\end{array}\right), \quad x=\binom{c}{d}, \quad \text { and } \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{c}
y_{1} \\
y_{2} \\
\vdots \\
y_{m}
\end{array}\right)
$$

then it follows that $E=\|y-A x\|^{2}$.
We develop a general method for finding an explicit vector $x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$ that minimizes $E$; that is, given an $m \times n$ matrix $A$, we find $x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$ such that $\left\|y-A x_{0}\right\| \leq\|y-A x\|$ for all vectors $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$. This method not only allows us to find the linear function that best fits the data, but also, for any positive integer $k$, the best fit using a polynomial of degree at most $k$.

First, we need some notation and two simple lemmas. For $x, y \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$, let $\langle x, y\rangle_{n}$ denote the standard inmer product of $x$ and $y$ in $\mathrm{F}^{n}$. Recall that if $x$ and $y$ are regarled as column vectors, then $\langle x, y\rangle_{n}=y^{*} x$.

Lemma 1. Let $A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F), x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$, and $y \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}$. Then

$$
\langle A x, y\rangle_{m}=\left\langle x, A^{*} y\right\rangle_{n}
$$

Proof. By a generalization of the corollary to Theorem 6.11 (see Exercise $5(\mathrm{~b}))$. we have

$$
\langle A x, y\rangle_{m}=y^{*}(A x)=\left(y^{*} A\right) \cdot x=\left(A^{*} y\right)^{*} \cdot x=\left\langle x \cdot A^{*} y\right\rangle_{n}
$$

Lemma 2. Let $A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)$. Then $\operatorname{rank}\left(A^{*} A\right)=\operatorname{rank}(A)$.

Proof. By the dimension theorem. we nerd only show that, for $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$, we have $A^{*} A x=0$ if and only if $A x=0$. (loarly. $A x=0$ implies that $A^{*} A x=0$. So assume that $A^{*} A x=0$. Then

$$
0=\left\langle A^{*} A r, x\right\rangle_{n}=\left\langle A x, A^{* *} \cdot r\right\rangle_{m}=\langle A x, A x\rangle_{m}
$$

so that $A x=0$.
Corollary. If $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix such that $\operatorname{rank}(A)=n$. then $A^{*} A$ is invertible.

Now let $A$ be an $m \times n$ matrix and $y \in \mathrm{~F}^{\prime \prime}$. Dodine $\mathrm{W}=\left\{A x: x \in \mathrm{~F}^{\prime}\right\}$ : that is, $W=R\left(L_{A}\right)$. By the corollary to Theorem 6.6 ( p .350 ), there exists a unique vector in $W$ that is closest to $y$. Call this vector $A x_{0}$, where $r_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$. Then $\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\| \leq\|A x-y\|$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$; so .rol has the property that $E=\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\|$ is minimal, as desired.

To develop a practical method for finding such an $r_{0}$, we note from The-
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_02_09_645a3628639374fdb486g-374.jpg?height=46&width=1167&top_left_y=1510&top_left_x=71) all $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$. Thus, by Lemma 1, we have that $\left\langle x, A^{*}\left(A r_{0}-y\right)\right\rangle_{n}=0$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$; that is, $A^{*}\left(A x_{0}-y\right)=0$. So we need only find a solution $x_{0}$ to $A^{*} A x=A^{*} y$. If, in addition, we assume that $\operatorname{rank}(A)=n$, then by Lemma 2 we have $x_{0}=\left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1} A^{*} y$. We summarize this discussion in the following theorem.

Theorem 6.12. Let $A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)$ and $y \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}$. Then there exists $x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$ such that $\left(A^{*} A\right) x_{0}=A^{*} y$ and $\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\| \leq\|A . r-y\|$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}$. Furthermore, if $\operatorname{rank}(A)=n$, then $x_{0}=\left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1} A^{*} y$.

To return to our experimenter, let us suppose that the data collected are $(1,2),(2,3),(3.5)$, and $(4,7)$. Then

$$
A=\left(\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
2 & 1 \\
3 & 1 \\
4 & 1
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{l}
2 \\
3 \\
5 \\
7
\end{array}\right)
$$

hence

$$
A^{*} A=\left(\begin{array}{llll}
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
2 & 1 \\
3 & 1 \\
4 & 1
\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{rr}
30 & 10 \\
10 & 4
\end{array}\right)
$$

Thus

$$
\left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\begin{array}{rr}
4 & -10 \\
-10 & 30
\end{array}\right) .
$$

Therefore

$$
\binom{c}{d}=x_{0}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\begin{array}{rr}
4 & -10 \\
-10 & 30
\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{llll}
1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}
2 \\
3 \\
5 \\
7
\end{array}\right)=\binom{1.7}{0} .
$$

It follows that the line $y=1.71$ is the least spuares line. The error $E$ paty be computed directly as $\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\|^{2}=0.3$.

Suppose that the experimenter chose the times $t_{i}(1 \leq i \leq m)$ to satisfy

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i}=0
$$

Then the two columes of $A$ wond be orthogonal. so $A^{*} A$ would be a diagonal matrix (see Exercise 19). In this case, the computations are greatly simplified.

In practice, the $m \times 2$ matrix $A$ in our least squares application has rank equal to two, and hence $A^{*} A$ is invertible by the corollary to Lemmat 2 . For, otherwise, the first column of $A$ is a multiple of the second column. which consists only of ones. But this wonld occur only if the experimenter collects all the data at exactly one time.

Finally, the method above may also be applied if, for some $k$, the experimenter wants to fit a polynomial of degree at most $k$ to the data. ' For instance. if a polynomial $y=c t^{2}+d t+e$ of degree at most 2 is desired, the appropriate model is

$$
r=\left(\begin{array}{c}
r \\
d \\
r
\end{array}\right), \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{c}
y_{1} \\
y_{2} \\
\vdots \\
y_{m}
\end{array}\right), \quad \text { andl } \quad A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}
t_{1}^{2} & t_{1} & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
l_{m}^{2} & t_{m} & 1
\end{array}\right) .
$$

## Minimal Solutions to Systems of Linear Equations

Even when a system of linear equations $A x=b$ is consistent, there may be no unique solution. In such cases, it may be desirable to find a solution of minimal norm. A solution $s$ to $A x=b$ is called a minimal solution if $\|s\| \leq$ $\|u\|$ for all other solutions $u$. The next theorem assures that every consistent system of linear equations has a unique minimal solution and provides a method for computing it.

Theorem 6.13. Let $A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)$ and $b \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}$. Suppose that $A x=b$ is consistent. Then the following statements are true.
(a) There exists exactly one minimal solution $s$ of $A x=b$, and $s \in \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right)$.
(b) The vector $s$ is the only solution to $A x=b$ that lies in $\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A} \cdot\right)$; that is, if $u$ satisfices $\left(A A^{*}\right) u=b$, then $s=A^{*} u$.

Proof. (a) For simplicity of notation, we let $W=R\left(L_{A^{*}}\right)$ and $W^{\prime}=N\left(L_{A}\right)$. Let $x$ be any solution to $A x=b$. By Theorem 6.6 (p. 350), $x=s+y$ for some $s \in \mathrm{~W}$ and $y \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}$. But $\mathrm{W}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}^{\prime}$ by Exercise 12, and therefore $b=A x=A s+A y=A s$. So $s$ is a solution to $A x=b$ that lies in W. To prove (a), we need only show that $s$ is the unique minimal solution. Let $v$ be any solution to $A x=b$. By Theorem 3.9 (p. 172), we have that $v=s+u$, where $u \in \mathrm{~W}^{\prime}$. Since $s \in \mathrm{~W}$, which equals $\mathrm{W}^{\perp}$ by Exercise 12, we have

$$
\because \quad\|v\|^{2}=\|s+u\|^{2}=\|s\|^{2}+\|u\|^{2} \geq\|s\|^{2}
$$

by Exercise 10 of Section 6.1. Thus $s$ is a minimal solution. We can also see from the preceding calculation that if $\|v\|=\|s\|$, then $u=0$; hence $v=s$. Therefore $s$ is the unique minimal solution to $A x=b$, proving (a).
(b) Assume that $v$ is also a solution to $A x=b$ that lies in W . Then

$$
v-s \in W \cap W^{\prime}=W \cap W^{\perp}=\{0\} ;
$$

so $v=s$.
Finally, suppose that $\left(A A^{*}\right) u=b$. and let $v=A^{*} u$. Then $v \in \mathrm{~W}$ and $A v=b$. Therefore $s=v=A^{*} u$ by the discussion above.

## Example 3

Consider the system

$$
\begin{aligned}
x+2 y+z & =4 \\
x-y+2 z & =-11 \\
x+5 y & =19 .
\end{aligned}
$$

Let

$$
A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr}
1 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & -1 & 2 \\
1 & 5 & 0
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad b=\left(\begin{array}{r}
4 \\
-11 \\
19
\end{array}\right)
$$

To find the minimal solution to this system, we must first find some solution $u$ to $A A^{*} x=b$. Now

$$
A A^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{rrr}
6 & 1 & 11 \\
1 & 6 & -4 \\
11 & -4 & 26
\end{array}\right) ;
$$

so we consider the system

$$
\begin{aligned}
6 x+y+11 z= & 4 \\
x+6 y-4 z= & -11 \\
11 x-4 y+26 z= & 19
\end{aligned}
$$

for which one solution is

$$
u=\left(\begin{array}{r}
1 \\
-2 \\
0
\end{array}\right)
$$

(Any solution will suffice.) Hence

$$
s=A^{*} u=\left(\begin{array}{r}
-1 \\
4 \\
-3
\end{array}\right)
$$

is the minimal solution to the given system.

## EXERCISES

1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying imer product spaces are finite-dimensional.
(a) Every lincar operator has an adjoint.
(b) Every lincar operator on $V$ has the form $x \rightarrow\langle x, y\rangle$ for some $y \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(c) For every lincar operator T on V and every ordered basis 3 for V , we have $\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{a}=\left([\mathrm{T}]_{i}\right)^{*}$.
(d) The adjoint of a linear operator is mique.
(e) For any lincor operators T and U and scalars $a$ and $b$,

$$
(a \mathrm{~T}+b \mathrm{U})^{*}=a \mathrm{~T}^{*}+b \mathrm{U}^{*}
$$

(f) For any $n \times n$ matrix $A$. we lave $\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}$.
(g) For any linear operator T , we have $\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{T}$.
2. For each of the following inner prochuct spaces $V$ (over $F$ ) and linear transformations $\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F$, find a vector $y$ surl that $\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle$ for all $x \in \mathrm{~V}$.
(a) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{3}, \mathrm{~g}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right)=a_{1}-2 a_{2}+4 a_{3}$
(b) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{2}, \mathrm{~g}\left(\tilde{\sim}_{1}, z_{2}\right)=z_{1}-2 z_{2}$
(c) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)$ with $\langle f, h\rangle=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{l}} f(t) h(t) d t, \mathrm{~g}(f)=f(0)+f^{\prime}(1)$
3. For each of the following inner product spaces $V$ and linear operators $T$ on $V$, evaluate $T^{*}$ at the given vector in $V$.
(a) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{2}, \mathrm{~T}(a, b)=(2 a+b, a-3 b), x=(3,5)$.
(b) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{2}, \mathrm{~T}\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)=\left(2 z_{1}+i z_{2},(1-i) z_{1}\right), x=(3-i .1+2 i)$.
(c) $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)$ with $\langle f: g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t . \mathrm{T}(f)=f^{\prime}+3 f$, $f(t)=4-2 t$
